mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive engineering analysis of energy systems covering primary fuels including nuclear (A.1), renewable sources including solar, wind, water, and biomass (A.2), and fossil fuels: coal, natural gas, oil (A.3). Addresses conversion technologies and distribution to end users (B, C), heat exchangers and refrigeration (B.7), nuclear energy devices (B.9), solar energy devices (B.10), batteries and fuel cells (C.4), and electric power generation (C.7).

Nuclear fission and fusion technologies as primary energy sources (A.1). Covers nuclear fission reactors and fusion research as devices for utilizing nuclear energy (B.9), and nuclear-powered generators for electric power generation (C.7).

Solar photovoltaics, wind power, hydropower, and biomass as recurring energy sources (A.2). Wind and water turbines as devices for utilizing wind and water energy (B.2). Solar energy devices (B.10). Battery storage technologies (C.4) and grid-connected electricity generation including photovoltaic devices (C.7).

Nuclear physics from radioactivity through quantum chromodynamics, covering atomic structure and nuclear stability as foundations for primary energy sources through fission and radioactivity (A.1), with practical applications in medicine connecting to nuclear energy utilization (B.9).

Radioactivity covering nuclear fission, fusion, and radioisotopes as primary energy sources (A.1). Addresses the harnessing of nuclear power for energy production through fission reactors and radioactive isotopes (B.9).

Fundamental laws governing heat, energy conversion, and the directionality of natural processes, directly relevant to heat exchangers and thermal devices (B.7). Covers thermoelectric concepts and energy conversion principles (C.1).

Engineering practice harnessing natural phenomena including electromagnetism and gravity for human needs (B). Covers muscle-powered mechanisms and simple machines like levers (B.1), electric motors and electromagnetic devices (B.5.b), and the broad application of energy conversion technologies (C).

Physics of light covering lasers, optical technologies, and practical applications in DVDs and domestic contexts as lamps and lighting devices (C.5). Quantum light sources for communications connecting to optoelectronic devices including semiconductor lasers and optical fibres (D.3).

Magnetism covering electromagnets (B.5.a) and electric motors including electromagnetic theory from Faraday, Tesla, and Maxwell (B.5.b). Addresses electrical power generation through motors, generators, and turbines as devices for utilizing magnetic and electrical energy (B.5, C.7).

States of matter including liquid crystals, superconductors, and ferromagnets with practical applications in silicon chips and liquid crystal displays as solid-state devices (D.2). Covers semiconductor technology and optoelectronic applications (D.3).
Fluid mechanics studying liquids and gases in motion and at rest, with engineering applications directly relevant to hydraulic devices utilizing fluid pressure and flow (B.4.d).

Thermodynamics including entropy and energy conversion at the molecular level relevant to thermoelectric phenomena (C.1). Covers electrochemistry and reaction kinetics connecting to batteries and fuel cells (C.4).
Solar physics covering thermonuclear reactions as the Sun's primary energy source (A.1), and solar energy as a recurring energy source driving life and technology on Earth (A.2, B.10).
Nuclear fission and fusion as energy sources for weapons (A.1). Covers the development from atomic bombs through thermonuclear devices, relating to chemical and nuclear explosives (B.8.b).

Entropy and thermodynamics with practical applications in the energy transition including heat pumps, solar cells, and carbon capture -- relevant to heat exchangers and refrigeration (B.7) and thermoelectric devices (C.1).

Chemical elements from ancient theory to modern transuranic synthesis, covering atomic structure, isotopes, and nuclear science including heavy water -- relevant to primary energy sources through nuclear reactions (A.1).
